entities.name: saṃjñā
entities.cite: the samjna before its connection to the samjnin is rupa-padarthika

entities.name: rūpa
entities.cite: the name has the form itself as its meaning

axioms.name: rupa-padarthika
axioms.cite: the samjna before its connection to the samjnin is rupa-padarthika — its meaning is just the form

axioms.name: svatantra
axioms.cite: the name has a standing of its own prior to the assignment

denials.name: parinamate
denials.cite: designation is a relation between two reals, not an absorption of one into the other

claims.name: artha
claims.cite: the name has the form itself as its meaning

claims.name: nimitta
claims.cite: becomes fit to be the occasion for both the sixth case and the first

rejected_readings.label: name_as_property
rejected_readings.rendering: The name is merely a property or quality of phonetic forms, without standing as an independent linguistic item.
rejected_readings.why: Demotes samjna from linguisticItem to property, which contradicts the axiom that samjna has svatantra (standing of its own). A property cannot have the kind of autonomy the verse describes.

rejected_readings.label: designation_absorption
rejected_readings.rendering: When the name is connected to what it names, the name merges with and is absorbed into the object, losing its own nature.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts parinamate (real transformation/absorption) which the commentary explicitly rejects. Designation is a relation preserving both reals, not an absorption of the name into the named.

notes: The verse formalizes the ontological basis of grammatical usage: the name (samjna) maintains logical priority as its own linguistic item with the form as meaning (rupa as artha), and this autonomy is preserved when it enters abhisambandha with what it names (samjnin). Both nominative and genitive constructions are licensed because the name is the nimitta (occasion) for designation. The philosophical cargo echoes 1.65: designation is relationality between two independent reals.
